questionText: When counting conductors for box fill, how are conductors that pass through the box without splice counted?
explanation: Per NEC 314.16(B)(1), conductors that pass through a box without splice or termination count as one conductor.
sparkyTip: Pass-through wires count as ONE, not two! They enter and exit but only take up one conductor's worth of space.

questionText: What is the volume allowance for internal cable clamps in a box?
options: One volume allowance for each clamp
A single volume allowance for all clamps combined
Two volume allowances for all clamps
explanation: Per NEC 314.16(B)(2), a single volume allowance based on the largest conductor in the box shall be made for all cable clamps combined, whether one or more.
sparkyTip: All clamps together = just ONE volume allowance. Doesn't matter if you have 1 clamp or 5!

questionText: Pigtails (short conductors for splicing) originating and terminating within the box are counted as:
explanation: Per NEC 314.16(B)(1), conductors that originate within the box and do not leave (such as pigtails) are not counted for box fill.
sparkyTip: Pigtails are FREE! If it starts AND ends inside the box, it doesn't count toward fill.

questionText: Luminaire (fixture) wires that enter a box from a domed canopy are:
options: Counted the same as branch circuit conductors
Not counted if 6 inches or less in length
explanation: Per NEC 314.16(B)(1) Exception, fixture wires from a domed luminaire or similar canopy are not counted if they are 6 inches or less in length.
sparkyTip: Short fixture wires (6 inches or less) from domed canopies get a free pass - no fill count!

questionText: How many #12 AWG conductors can fit in an 18 cubic inch box with no devices, clamps, or grounds?
explanation: 18 cubic inches ÷ 2.25 cubic inches per #12 conductor = 8 conductors maximum.
sparkyTip: Simple division: Box volume ÷ conductor volume = max conductors. 18 ÷ 2.25 = 8!

questionText: A box contains 6 current-carrying 12 AWG conductors, 3 equipment grounds, one internal clamp, and one device yoke. Using 2.25 cu in per 12 AWG conductor, what is the minimum required box volume?
explanation: Count conductors: 6 + grounds(1) + clamp(1) + device yoke(2) = 10 equivalents. Volume = 10 x 2.25 = 22.5 cu in.
sparkyTip: All grounds count as one; device yokes count as two.

questionText: An electrician installs a 3/4-inch Type C conduit body in the middle of a long straight run of EMT. Under what condition is the electrician permitted to make wire splices inside this conduit body?
options: Only if the conduit body is supported within 3 feet.
Only if the manufacturer has durably marked the cubic-inch capacity on the conduit body.
Splices are never permitted in a conduit body; they are strictly for pulling.
Only if the conductors are 4 AWG or larger.
explanation: Per NEC 314.16(C)(2), conduit bodies are allowed to contain splices, taps, or devices ONLY if the manufacturer has durably and legibly marked the cubic-inch capacity on the interior or exterior of the fitting. You must then calculate the box fill to ensure the splices don't exceed that stamped volume.
sparkyTip: Not all condulets are created equal! If you buy a cheap conduit body that doesn't have a volume stamp on the inside, it is legally just a pull-point. If you bury a wire nut in there, the inspector will make you rip it out!

questionText: Standard box fill calculations (cubic inch volume) are used for conductors size 6 AWG and smaller. Which NEC Article governs the minimum sizing requirements for junction boxes containing conductors 4 AWG and larger?
explanation: NEC 314.16 applies to boxes containing conductors 6 AWG and smaller (volume calculations). When conductors are 4 AWG or larger, the box sizing must comply with NEC 314.28, which dictates minimum length and width based on the size of the raceways (8x and 6x rules) to ensure proper wire bending space.
sparkyTip: Remember the cutoff: 6 AWG and smaller = cubic inch volume (314.16), 4 AWG and larger = 8x and 6x raceway rules (314.28).
